title: Egg washing, grading, and storage
subtitle: How to handle eggs from a small flock to keep them clean and extend their shelf life.
excerpt: A freshly laid egg from a clean nest box needs almost no intervention. The problem is nest boxes are not always clean, and not all eggs are laid when you are there to collect them. This is what you do with the eggs you find.
sourceNotes: Food Standards Agency, guidance on egg hygiene. DEFRA, Poultry Register and egg marketing standards. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive.

content.text: on a freshly laid egg is worth keeping when you can. A clean, bloom-intact egg keeps at room temperature for up to 10 days, longer in a cool pantry. Once the bloom is removed by washing, the egg needs refrigerating and will not keep as long.

The best defence against dirty eggs is frequent collection. Collect in the morning and again in the early afternoon; eggs left overnight in a busy nestbox attract interest from hens, accumulate droppings, and are more likely to be damaged. Keep nestboxes topped up with fresh bedding to reduce the contact area between eggs and the floor of the box.

For eggs with a small dry smear or light spot of mud or droppings: rub the spot off with a dry cloth, fine sandpaper, or a dedicated dry egg-cleaning pad. This removes the debris without breaking the bloom. The egg can then be stored unwashed. Do not scrub aggressively; fine scratching of the shell surface increases bacterial permeability.

Eggs that need full washing must be washed in water warmer than the egg, ideally around 40°C. Cold water causes the egg contents to contract slightly, pulling bacteria inward through the shell pores if the shell surface is contaminated. Hot water moves the flow in the other direction.

Wash quickly, dry immediately with a clean cloth, and refrigerate the egg straight away. Washed eggs should not be sold as Class A eggs under UK egg marketing standards; they are for home use or can be sold as Class B (baking and food manufacturing) if you produce commercially.

Run a thumb across the shell surface when you collect. A hairline crack is audible as a dull note when you tap the egg against another; cracked eggs are more resonant. Cracked eggs should be used within 24 hours and cooked thoroughly; they cannot be sold.

Grade by size if you are selling or if you bake to standard recipes: very large (over 73 g), large (63 to 73 g), medium (53 to 63 g), small (under 53 g). A kitchen scale and an egg carton is all the infrastructure needed for a small flock. For home use, any size is fine; a pullet's first eggs are always small and just as good.
to check for double yolks (common in young pullets) or blood spots if you want to identify them before cracking. Store point-down in a cool pantry or the main body of the fridge.
